text: The laparoscopic procedure begins with pneumoperitoneum established at 15 mmHg, using optical entry 20 cm from the xiphoid process in the supraumbilical region. Two secondary ports are placed on either side for instruments, and a lateral port in the left axillary line retracts the stomach. A Nathanson liver retractor is positioned in the epigastric region to expose the hiatus. The patient is positioned steep head-up, with the surgeon standing between the legs and the monitor at the head side. Hunter Graspers are used to pull down the contents of the large hernia defect, aiming to remove the entire sac. Dissection begins through the avascular gastrohepatic membrane using harmonic ACE, identifying the right crux. The hepatic branch of the vagus nerve is sacrificed due to prior cholecystectomy. Dissection proceeds carefully to avoid injury to the esophagus, excising the sac and placing it in an endo bag. Anterior and posterior dissection around the esophagus is performed to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length for a 360-degree wrap. Hemostasis is maintained, and care is taken not to injure the vagus nerve near the esophagus. Mobilization of short gastric vessels occurs as the left crux apex is approached.

The surgeon performs short gastric mobilization to reach the apex of the two crura, ensuring the esophagus and stomach attachments are freed using the harmonic ACE. The esophagus is mobilized both intra-abdominally and intra-thoracically up to the mediastinum, creating a retroesophageal window while preserving the posterior branch of the vagus nerve. The gastroesophageal junction is freed, and a 15 cm umbilical tape is placed around the esophagus for retraction, secured with surgeon's knots. Care is taken to avoid injury to the esophagus and vagus nerves, and to prevent bleeding near the aorta and pre-aortic fascia.

The surgeon removes the complete hernia sac and proceeds with crurography, mobilizing the right and left crura. Using a non-absorbable polyester suture on a 45 mm needle, the surgeon approximates the crura with U stitches to prevent muscle tearing. Three sutures are placed for complete approximation, ensuring the esophagus is not obstructed. The posterior crurography is performed, avoiding anterior crurography to prevent obstruction. A bougie is passed to test the tightness of the sutures, followed by placing a stay suture at the fundus of the stomach, visible at the highest portion, posterior to the gastrolytic area.

The surgeon performs the fundic wrap by using a silk suture as a guide, ensuring the stomach does not twist during the retroesophageal approach. The fundus is pulled to the right side, and the shoe shine maneuver is executed to prevent slippage. Serocerosal gastrogastric sutures are placed on the wrap, including the esophagus in the first suture to secure the wrap postoperatively. Care is taken to avoid injury to surrounding structures such as the liver, IVC, aorta, esophagus, and stomach. Three sutures are placed to create a floppy wrap of approximately three to four centimeters, ensuring it remains retroesophageal rather than retrogastric.

The surgeon performs gastro-gastric suturing, typically three sutures, to secure the wrap. Right and left cruropexy are conducted to prevent the wrap from migrating intrathoracically post-operatively. The ports, including the Nathanson port, are removed under direct vision. The patient is extubated on the table to minimize post-operative retching. An x-ray chest is performed in the recovery room, followed by a gastrographin study to ensure no leaks and proper wrap positioning.
